results.possibleIdentity: Joseph T. Rigo was a prominent technical writer, manager, and influential figure in the field of technical communication and computer documentation during the late 1960s and 1970s. Based in New York, New York, he was a manager at IBM and a prolific columnist for Computerworld, where he frequently wrote on the professionalization of technical writing, the management of programmer activities, and the governance of the Association for Computing Machinery (ACM). He was a key figure in the establishment of professional standards for software documentation. In recognition of his significant contributions to the field, the ACM Special Interest Group on Design of Communication (SIGDOC) established the Joseph T. Rigo Award in his honor. The award is presented annually to individuals who have made a significant impact on the field of documentation through research, practical work, or authorship. He is also identified as a 1956 graduate of the University of Maine and was active in the New York alumni community. His work often bridged technical fields such as computer programming and systems documentation with the broader challenges of technical communication in the burgeoning computer industry.
